coredata.dc:title: A near-infrared and two-photon ratiometric fluorescent probe with a large Stokes shift for sulfur dioxide derivatives detection and its applications in vitro and in vivo
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yuping
Ma, Yanyan
Lin, Weiying
coredata.dc:description: As a critical gaseous signaling molecule, SO2 is involved in multiple physiological and pathological processes. However, the pathogenesis of many serious diseases including lung cancer, cardiovascular diseases and neurological disorders caused by excessive inhalation of SO2 is still unknown. Hence, the development of reliable analytical methods for efficiently and specifically monitoring SO2 in living systems is of significance. In this work, a novel near-infrared and two-photon ratiometric fluorescent probe NIR-SO2-TP was first proposed for optical SO2 sensing. The newly synthesized fluorescent probe NIR-SO2-TP, which is based on coumarin-benzopyrylium π-conjugated platform, exhibited high selectivity, rapid response time, lower detection limit and a large Stokes shift (185 nm). Importantly, the novel probe has been successfully applied to sense SO2 in living cells, tissues, zebrafish and living mice at both near-infrared and two-photon modes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur dioxide
Two-photon
Near-infrared
Large Stokes shift
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of probe NIR-SO2-TP to SO2
Cell cytotoxicity assay and cell imaging
3D fluorescence imaging of liver tissues from mice

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The structure and proposed mechanism of NIR-SO2-TP for response to NaHSO3.
The synthesis route of the probe NIR-SO2-TP.

(a) Absorption profile of NIR-SO2-TP (10 μM) in the absence and presence of NaHSO3 (50 μM). (b) Fluorescence spectra of NIR-SO2-TP (10 μM) with the treatment of various concentrations of NaHSO3 (0–50 μM). λex = 440 nm. (c) The absorption (black) and fluorescence (red) spectra of NIR-SO2-TP (10 μM). (d) Time-dependent fluorescence response of NIR-SO2-TP (10 μM) treated with NaHSO3 (50 μM), recording interval: 3 s. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Partial 1H NMR spectra of NIR-SO2-TP and NIR-SO2-TP treated with NaHSO3 in DMSO-d 6 -D2O.

Fluorescence signal changes of the probe NIR-SO2-TP (10 μM) and NIR-SO2-TP treated with HSO3 − (50 μM) at different pH values for 30 min. (b) Fluorescence responses of the probe NIR-SO2-TP (10 μM) in the presence of a series of different relevant species. Analytes: (1) only NIR-SO2-TP; (2) NaClO; (3) TBHP; (4) CuSO4; (5) Fe2(SO4)3; (6) KI; (7) NaNO2; (8) MgCl2; (9) Na2SO3; (10) KNO3; (11) NaF; (12) Na2S; (13) ZnSO4; (14) AgNO3; (15) DTBP; (16) FeSO4; (17) H2O2; (18) Hcy; (19) SnCl2; (20) GSH; (21) Cys; (22) CoCl2; (23) CaCl2; (24) NaHS; (25) NaHSO3.

(A) Images of HeLa cells stained with NIR-SO2-TP (10 μM) in the absence and presence of NaHSO3 (50 μM) at one-photon (a – d) and two-photon (e) modes. (a1 – e1) Images of living cells cultured with NIR-SO2-TP. (a2 – e2) Images of HeLa cells co-incubated with NaHSO3 and NIR-SO2-TP. (B) Quantified relative fluorescence intensities for (A) at OP mode. (C) Quantified relative fluorescence intensities for (A) at TP mode. Scale bar = 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

3D fluorescence images of the liver slices from mice treated with 20 μM NIR-SO2-TP in the absence (a1, a3) and presence (b1, b3) of 100 μM NaHSO3. (a2) and (b2) are the images of green channel in TP mode. (a4) and (b4) are the images of red channel in NIR mode. Scale bar: 25 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) One-photon (a – d) and two-photon (e) images of zebrafish stained with NIR-SO2-TP (20 μM) in the absence and presence of NaHSO3 (100 μM). (a2 – e2) Images of zebrafish cultured with NaHSO3 and NIR-SO2-TP. (B) Quantified relative fluorescence intensities for (A) at OP mode. (C) Quantified relative fluorescence intensities for (A) at TP mode. Scale bar = 100 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

In vivo fluorescence imaging of NIR-SO2-TP in the living mice. (a) mice only; (b) Fluorescence images of mice injected into 100 μL 20 μM NIR-SO2-TP; (c – f) Fluorescence images of mice further injected into 50 μL 100 μM NaHSO3, recorded at 15 S, 30 S, 45 S, 60 S respectively; λex = 620 nm, λem = 845 nm..

head.ce:title: A near-infrared and two-photon ratiometric fluorescent probe with a large Stokes shift for sulfur dioxide derivatives detection and its applications in vitro and in vivo

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Fluorescent Probes for Biological Imaging, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong, 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Fluorescent Probes for Biological Imaging
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new near-infrared and two-photon fluorescentt probe with a large Stokes shift was engineered to detect SO2 derivatives in living cells and mice.

As a critical gaseous signaling molecule, SO2 is involved in multiple physiological and pathological processes. However, the pathogenesis of many serious diseases including lung cancer, cardiovascular diseases and neurological disorders caused by excessive inhalation of SO2 is still unknown. Hence, the development of reliable analytical methods for efficiently and specifically monitoring SO2 in living systems is of significance. In this work, a novel near-infrared and two-photon ratiometric fluorescent probe NIR-SO2-TP was first proposed for optical SO2 sensing. The newly synthesized fluorescent probe NIR-SO2-TP, which is based on coumarin-benzopyrylium π-conjugated platform, exhibited high selectivity, rapid response time, lower detection limit and a large Stokes shift (185 nm). Importantly, the novel probe has been successfully applied to sense SO2 in living cells, tissues, zebrafish and living mice at both near-infrared and two-photon modes.

ce:section.ce:para: SO2, a well-known gaseous pollutant, was found to have unique physiological and biochemical functions in living organisms. For example, it has been proved that SO2 can regulate vascular smooth muscle tone in concert with NO and functionalize as a novel gas messenger molecule like NO and CO [1–3]. In addition, SO2 can be produced endogenously from sulphur-containing amino acids in the body [4–6], which is most likely hydrated to the formation of its derivatives of sulfite (SO3 −) and bisulfite (HSO3 −) when it is dissolved in water solution or inhaled into the body through the respiratory system [7,8]. Thus, bisulfite is always applied to survey the biological activities of SO2 and recognized as a vasoactive factor [9,10]. However, epidemiological studies have shown that the excessive inhalation of SO2 not only causes damage to the respiratory system [11], but also causes vascular disease [12,13], lung cancer [14,15], and nerve disorder [16–18]. Therefore, to entirely perceive the physiological and pathological processes of SO2 and its derivatives, the development of highly selective and sensitive methods for detecting sulfite or bisulfite in biological systems is particularly important.

Until now, multiple detection methods like spectrophotometric method [19], chromatographic detection [20], electrochemical analysis [21], chemiluminescence [22] and fluorescent probes [23] have been applied to sense SO2 or its derivatives. Among all these methods, fluorescent probes have been evaluated as powerful tools to detect biological agents due to their high selectivity and sensitivity, real-time detection and easiness of manipulating [24–28]. Recently, a considerable amount of fluorescent probes for sensing SO2 and its derivatives have been engineered [29–35]. However, there still exist several thorny questions for these probes. First, they are usually assessed by one-photon (OP) mode, which possessing short emission shift will generate self-quenching and measurement deviation. In contrast, two-photon (TP) dyes not only overcome the above mentioned shortages, but also have advantages including deeper tissue penetration, high three-dimensional resolution and especially giant Stokes shift. Second, although some excellent probes have emission wavelength around near-infrared (NIR) range, few of them are utilized with NIR excitation. Therefore, it is necessary to propose a new probe with the both advantages of TP and NIR for sensing SO2 in vitro and in vivo.

In this work, we reported a new NIR and TP ratiometric probe NIR-SO2-TP based on the Michael-Addition reaction for sensing SO2. The probe NIR-SO2-TP utilized benzopyrylium moiety as reaction site for sensing SO2, and incorporated it with the coumarin to extend π-conjugation. This probe can be excited at 650 nm with a emission range around 790–900 nm and release satisfactory TP profile of coumarin when exposed to SO2 as anticipated. In addition, the probe exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity toward SO2 with rapid response, low detection limit and large Stokes shift. Significantly, the novel probe was triumphantly utilized for imaging SO2 in cells, tissues, zebrafish and mice with simultaneous NIR and TP modes.

All chemicals and solvents involved in this study were purchased from commercial suppliers and used without further purification. The instruments involved in this research were declared in supporting information.

The stock solution of NIR-SO2-TP (1.0 mM) was prepared in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). NaHSO3 was dissolved in distilled water to prepare stock solution of 10 mM. The multiple species stock solutions (AgNO3, Cys, CoCl2, CaCl2, CuSO4, Fe2(SO4)3, Na2SO3, FeSO4, NaHS, KNO3, ZnSO4, KI, MgCl2, Na2SO4, SnCl2, Hcy, NaF, NaClO, SnCl2, NaNO2, Na2S, tert-Butyl hydroperoxide (TBHP), H2O2, GSH) were prepared in distilled water at 100 mM, and the ditertbutylperoxide (DTBP) was dissolved in DMSO to prepare the stock solution (100 mM). The test solution was obtained by placing the requisite amount of the probe NIR-SO2-TP solution (10 μM), NaHSO3 solution (50 μM) in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4, 20% CH3CN).

A mixture of compound 1 (256 mg, 1 mmol) and compound 3 (305 mg, 1 mmol) were added to 30 mL ethanol and then 100 μL trifluoroacetic acid was added dropwise to the above solution. Then the mixture was refluxed for 8 h. After the temperature cooled down to 25 °C, the solvent was evaporated and the residue was purified by silica gel column chromatography (CH2Cl2/methanol = 20:1) to afford a green black powder of NIR-SO2-TP. The yield is 65%. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO) δ 8.48 (s, 2H), 8.35 (d, J = 11.9 Hz, 1H), 7.95 (d, J = 11.1 Hz, 1H), 7.88 (d, J = 9.3 Hz, 1H), 7.68 (d, J = 8.6 Hz, 1H), 7.47 (d, J = 9.0 Hz, 1H), 7.23 (s, 1H), 6.82 (d, J = 8.3 Hz, 1H), 6.61 (s, 1H), 5.33 (s, 1H), 3.76 (d, J = 7.0 Hz, 4H), 3.50 (d, J = 7.0 Hz, 4H), 2.87 (s, 2H), 2.80 (s, 2H), 2.68 (s, 1H), 2.34 (s, 1H), 2.00 (d, J = 7.8 Hz, 1H), 1.91 (s, 2H), 1.27 (t, J = 7.0 Hz, 6H), 1.16 (t, J = 6.9 Hz, 6H). 13C NMR (100 MHz, DMSO) δ 161.19, 156.30, 156.04, 152.51, 147.71, 143.63, 136.97, 132.27, 131.52, 131.35, 129.91, 124.64, 123.07, 119.65, 118.74, 113.42, 111.07, 110.61, 108.74, 96.02, 95.58, 46.07, 44.88, 27.57, 26.18, 20.88, 12.76. HRMS (m/z): [M+H]+ calcd for C33H36ClN2O3 +, 543.2487; found, 543.2403.

HeLa cells were manipulated in a sterile environment and were initially seeded into cultureplate for incubation of 24 h in cell culture fluid. Prior to the imaging experimental analysis, the cells were washed three times with prewarmed phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) buffer. Next, the HeLa cells were bred with NIR-SO2-TP (10 μM) for 0.5 h. Meanwhile, the other group cells were incubated with NaHSO3 (50 μM) for 0.5 h, then bred with the probe NIR-SO2-TP for another 0.5 h. The OP fluorescence images of cells were obtained at the emission ranges of 500–550 nm (λex = 488 nm) and 700–800 nm (λex = 633 nm) separately. The TP fluorescence images were obtained at 500–550 nm under excitation of 760 nm.

One group of zebrafish was cultured with 20 μM NIR-SO2-TP for 0.5 h. The other group was co-incubated with 100 μM NaHSO3 for 0.5 h, and then treated with 20 μM NIR-SO2-TP for further 0.5 h. The OP fluorescence images of zebrafish were obtained at the emission ranges of 500–550 nm (λex = 488 nm) and 700–800 nm (λex = 633 nm) separately. The TP fluorescence emission were obtained at 500–550 nm under excitation of 760 nm.

All the research here was supported by the Animal Ethical Experimentation Committee of Shandong University according to the requirements of the National Act on the use of experimental animals (China). For living mice imaging experiments, the mice were anesthetized by intraperitoneally injection of chloral hydrate 150 μL (4% in water). Then 100 μL 20 μM NIR-SO2-TP was injected into the mice intraperitoneally, followed by 50 μL 100 μM NaHSO3. The mice were imaged by the PerkinElmer IVIS Lumina Series III in vivo imaging system.

Recently, a new strategy for hybridization of two classical dyes was occasionally utilized to form a novel long-wavelength pigment with advantages of high quantum yields, long absorption wavelength, and low background interference [36–39]. Considering the striking photochemical and phoptophysical properties of coumarin, such as large Stokes shift, high quantum yield and typical TP profile, we designed it as the fluorophore of the new probe initially. Subsequently, inspired by the previous elegant reported probes [40,41], we utilized the electrophile of benzopyrylium as a response site to HSO3 −. Consequently, we decided to make the novel probe bonding coumarin group with benzopyrylium salt by π-conjugated bridge to form a NIR probe NIR-SO2-TP. As a result, it might enable the possibility for the new probe to work as a bio/chemosensor with both NIR and TP properties for detection of SO2. When HSO3 − attacked the reaction site, the π-conjugated platform of benzopyrylium unit would cut off based on the Michael-Addition reaction. In consequence, the green emission wavelength of a classical two-photon fluorophore of the coumarin would be released. (Scheme 1 ).

The whole synthesis route of NIR-SO2-TP was conducted as shown in Scheme 2 . Condensation of 4-diethylamino-salicylaldehyde and cyclohexanone in conc. H2SO4 gave compound 1. Meanwhile, condensation of 4-diethylamino-salicylaldehyde and ethyl acetoacetate with piperidine in ethanol afforded compound 2, which was then mixed with DMF and POCl3 to provide the essential intermediate 3 under nitrogen. NIR-SO2-TP was ultimately obtained by the hybridization of 1 and 3 with trifluoroacetic acid in ethanol. The characterizations of NIR-SO2-TP were conducted respectively by 1H NMR, 13C NMR and high-resolution mass spectroscopy (HRMS, Figs. S6–S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of probe NIR-SO2-TP to SO2
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effects of SO2 of the new probe NIR-SO2-TP to SO2, the spectral properties of the probe NIR-SO2-TP in solution were tested. We first investigated the UV–vis absorption of NIR-SO2-TP. As revealed in Fig. 1 a, the 10 μM probe NIR-SO2-TP in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4, 20% CH3CN) showed an intensive peak at 650 nm. Upon the treatment of NaHSO3 (50 μM), the absorption exhibited a descending at 650 nm and a strong increase at 440 nm. As proposed, the free probe NIR-SO2-TP (10 μM) exhibited NIR fluorescence emission peak at 835 nm. Delightfully, as various concentrations of NaHSO3 (0–50 μM) added to the NIR-SO2-TP (10 μM) solutions, the fluorescence profile of NIR-SO2-TP gradually faded in NIR region centered at 830 nm while enhanced at 530 nm (Fig. 1b). In addition, the fluorescence value of the probe was saturated by 50 μM NaHSO3 and manifested a nice linear response with NaHSO3 concentration ranging from 0 to 20 μM, and the detection limit for SO2 were calculated to be 5.12 μM (λex = 650 nm, Fig. S1a) and 1.06 μM (λex = 440 nm, Fig. S1b) separately. Additionally, as displayed in Fig. 1c, the probe showed great Stokes shift of 185 nm due to the intra-molecular rotation and strong ICT effect [42], which enabled highly efficient separation of absorbance and emission maximally. Furthermore, time-dependent response assay of NIR-SO2-TP (10 μM) treated with NaHSO3 (50 μM) in 10 mM PBS buffer (pH 7.4, 20% CH3CN) were carried out (Fig. 1d). The results indicated that NIR-SO2-TP could quickly respond to NaHSO3 within 12 s and the fluorescence intensity decreased 7-fold at 835 nm but increased 27-fold at 545 nm simultaneously. Meanwhile, when the probe NIR-SO2-TP and NIR-SO2-TP-HSO3 − were subjected to 1 h irradiation, there was no obvious changes in fluorescent properties demonstrating that the probe has excellent photostability (Fig. S2). Thus, the probe NIR-SO2-TP may be liable to provide precise detection for response to SO2.

We then evaluated the pH effect to the probe NIR-SO2-TP in the absence and presence of NaHSO3 in various pH buffer. As described in Fig. 3 a, there was scarcely any variation of fluorescence intensity for the free probe at pH 3-11. After treated with NaHSO3, a distinct fluorescent signal increase was acquired over a wide pH range from 6 to 9, which demonstrates that NIR-SO2-TP has potential in physiological conditions for sensing SO2.

Highly selective response is also a necessary for efficiently detecting target species among complicated biological samples. Thus, the probe NIR-SO2-TP (10 μM) was treated with a series of different biologically relevant species. Upon the treatment of various competitive species (Ca2+, ClO−, TBHP, Cu2+, Fe3+, I−, NO2−, Mg2+, Cl−, SO3 2−, NO3 −, F−, Zn2+, Ag+, DTBP, Fe2+, H2O2, Hcy, Sn2+, GSH, Cys, S2−, Co2+, HS−) at 500 μM in 10 mM PBS buffer (pH 7.4, 20% CH3CN), the fluorescence intensity displayed scarcely any obvious change (Fig. 3b). While the probe was treated with NaHSO3 (50 μM), the fluorescence intensity exhibited a quenching at emission of 835 nm and an obvious enhancement at emission of 530 nm. It confirmed that NIR-SO2-TP had great potential in specifically sensing SO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell cytotoxicity assay and cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: With the previous satisfactory fluorescence profile data of NIR-SO2-TP to SO2 in hand, we studied the response ability of NIR-SO2-TP to SO2 in cells and living animals. Before that, we conducted its specific toxicity of NIR-SO2-TP toward HeLa cells by MTT method. The data manifested that over 80% HeLa cells survived after 24 h cultured with probe (Fig. S5), implying that NIR-SO2-TP have low cytotoxicity to living cells.

Then, we studied the capacity of NIR-SO2-TP for visualizing SO2 in HeLa cells. As displayed in Fig. 4 , the cells cultured with the free probe NIR-SO2-TP (10 μM) for 0.5 h were mainly stained with NIR fluorescence at excitation of 633 nm in the red channel. In contrast, as the cells were exposed in the medium containing NIR-SO2-TP (10 μM) and NaHSO3 (50 μM), the former observed red fluorescence was quenched and the green fluorescence was intensively released at OP mode. Meanwhile, the cells also revealed strong green fluorescence of TP profile with excitation of 760 nm. These data sufficiently demonstrated that the probe NIR-SO2-TP has a capacity of imaging SO2 in living cells with the simultaneous NIR and TP fluorescence signals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3D fluorescence imaging of liver tissues from mice
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the favorable results acquired on fluorescence sensing of SO2 in cellular environment, subsequently we decided to utilize NIR-SO2-TP to detect SO2 inside liver tissues from mice. To monitor the tissue penetrating depth of the probe NIR-SO2-TP in the absence and presence of HSO3 −, the analysis data of liver tissues was recorded by Z-axis scanning and three-dimensional (3D) reconstruction. As shown in Fig. 5 , the mouse tissues cultured with 20 μM NIR-SO2-TP displayed NIR fluorescence penetration into the depth of 190 μm in the NIR manner. Compared to the NIR fluorescence, the green channel barely produced fluorescence signals at TP mode. However, when the liver slices treated with 20 μM NIR-SO2-TP and 100 μM NaHSO3, the fluorescence color changed from red to intensive green up to a penetration of 150 μm at TP mode. The above research indicated that NIR-SO2-TP is capable of visualizing SO2 in liver tissues at both NIR and TP modes.

To demonstrate that the fluorescence imaging of living animals can be realized for sensing SO2 by NIR-SO2-TP, the zebrafish were employed to investigate the capacity of NIR-SO2-TP to monitor SO2. As revealed in Fig. 6 , NIR fluorescence of the zebrafish which incubated with NIR-SO2-TP (20 μM) showed up under the excitation of 633 nm and green fluorescence signal was nearly obtained under OP excitation of 488 nm. However, with the addition of NaHSO3 (100 μM), a striking enhancement of fluorescence in the green channel at OP mode and a decline in the red channel were collected, confirming that SO2 could be specifically recognized by NIR-SO2-TP in zebrafish. Meanwhile, the enhanced fluorescent signals in the green channel were also collected after incubated with NaHSO3 at TP mode. The above research implied that NIR-SO2-TP is capable of entering zebrafish and sensing SO2 efficiently under NIR and TP excitation.

Benefited from a series of unique advantages of the near-infrared fluorescent probes, such as near-infrared emission, deep tissue penetration and lower background fluorescence interference, we finally sought to study in vivo imaging with NIR-SO2-TP for responce to SO2. Since the emission wavelength at about 530 nm is easily affected by autofluorescence of mice, we decided to focus on the emission of 845 nm under 620 nm excitation for in vivo imaging studies.

The mice was injected into NIR-SO2-TP (100 μL 20 μM) and incubated for 30 min. The NIR fluorescence was collected (Fig. 7 b) at an excitation wavelength of 620 nm and emission wavelength of 845 nm, indicating that the probe has capacity of in vivo imaging. Then, 100 μL 100 μM NaHSO3 was injected subcutaneously and the mice was imaged at an interval of 15 s. As shown in Fig. 7(c–f), a gradual quenching fluorescence was observed with the time increasing. These results demonstrated that NIR-SO2-TP could rapidly sense SO2 in live animal successfully.

In summary, we have designed and synthesized a new near-infrared and two-photon ratiometric fluorescent probe for SO2 sensing based on the Michael-Addition reaction. The probe NIR-SO2-TP exhibited NIR fluorescence initially of NIR emission (790–900 nm), and released green fluorescence of a classical two-photon fluorophore of the coumarin after treated with SO2. Moreover, the unique probe exhibited excellent selectivity toward SO2 with fast response and giant Stokes Shift (185 nm). Importantly, the probe NIR-SO2-TP was employed to monitor SO2 in living cells, tissues, zebrafish and mice at both TP (190 nm) and NIR (150 nm) modes. We anticipate that the new probe could be employed for dissecting the physiological and pathological processes of SO2 in complex living organisms.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by NSFC (21472067, 21672083, 21877048), Taishan Scholar Foundation (TS 201511041), and the startup fund of University of Jinan (309-10004).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.01.170.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Significance of endogenous sulphur-containing gases in the cardiovascular system
Sulfur dioxide upregulates the aortic nitric oxide pathway in rats
Sulfur dioxide preconditioning increases antioxidative capacity in rat with myocardial ischemia reperfusion (I/R) injury
L-Cysteine metabolism via 3-mercaptopyruvate pathway and sulfate formation in rat liver mitochondria
Ratiometric fluorescent sensing of copper ion based on chromaticity change strategy
Endogenously generated sulfur dioxide and its vasorelaxant effect in rats
Design of a molecular imprinting biosensor with multi-scale roughness for the detection across a broad spectrum of biomolecules
Generation of HSO3 − and OH radicals in SO3 2− reactions with inorganic environmental pollutants and its implications to SO3 2− toxicity
Dealing with methionine/homocysteine sulfur: cysteine metabolism to taurine and inorganic sulfur
iRGD-decorated red shift emissive carbon nanodots for tumor targeting fluorescence imaging
The vasorelaxant effect and its mechanisms of sodium bisulfite as a sulfur dioxide donor
Pulmonary impairment from chronic exposure to sulfur dioxide in a smelter
Hydrothermal synthesis of N, S co-doped carbon nanodots for highly selective detection of living cancer cells
Sulfite-stress induced functional and structural changes in the complexes of photosystems I and II in a cyanobacterium, Synechococcus elongatus PCC 7942
An ecologic analysis of county-level PM2.5 concentrations and lung cancer incidence and mortality
Mortality from lung cancer in workers exposed to sulfur dioxide in the pulp and paper industry
A polyaniline based ultrasensitive potentiometric immunosensor for cardiac troponin complex detection
SO2-induced neurotoxicity is mediated by cyclooxygenases-2-derived prostaglandin E2 and its downstream signaling pathway in rat hippocampal neurons
Dispersible and discrete metalloporphyrin-based CMP nanoparticles enabling colorimetric detection and quantitation of gaseous SO2
Determination of total sulfite in wine by ion chromatography after in-sample oxidation
Stabilized zirconia-based mixed potential type sensors utilizing MnNb2O6 sensing electrode for detection of low-concentration SO2
Improved chemiluminescence in fenton-like reaction via dodecylbenzene-sulfonate-intercalated layered double hydroxides
Imaging of colorectal cancers using activatable nanoprobes with second near-infrared window emission
Nanometer resolution imaging and tracking of fluorescent molecules with minimal photon fluxes
Small-molecule two-photon probes for bioimaging applications
Preparation of a Nile red-Pd-based fluorescent CO probe and its imaging applications in vitro and in vivo
Dual-site fluorescent probe for visualizing the metabolism of cys in living cells
A water-soluble near-infrared probe for colorimetric and ratiometric sensing of SO2 derivatives in living cells
Selective, highly sensitive fluorescent probe for the detection of sulfur dioxide derivatives in aqueous and biological environments
Fluorescent probes with dual-mode for rapid detection of SO2 derivatives in living cells: ratiometric and two-photon fluorescent sensors
A highly sulfite-selective ratiometric fluorescent probe based on ESIPT
Fluorescent probe for biological gas SO2 derivatives bisulfite and sulfite
A lysosome-targeted two-photon fluorescence probe for imaging of sulfur dioxide derivatives in living cells and zebrafish
“Quinoneephenol” transduction activated excited-state intramolecular proton transfer: a new strategy toward ratiometric fluorescent probe for sulfite in living cells
NIR-fluorescent coumarin-fused BODIPY dyes with large Stokes shifts

Two-photon and deep-red emission ratiometric fluorescent probe with a Large emission shift and signal ratios for sulfur dioxide: ultrafast response and applications in living cells, brain tissues, and zebrafishes
A mitochondria-targeted ratiometric fluorescent probe to monitor endogenously generated sulfur dioxide derivatives in living cells
Dual-site lysosome-targeted fluorescent probe for separate detection of endogenous biothiols and SO2 in living cells
A ratiometric fluorescence probe for selective detection of sulfite and its application in realistic samples
Rational development of near-infrared fluorophores with large Stokes shifts, bright one-photon, and two-photon emissions for bioimaging and biosensing applications
Molecular design of UV–vis absorption and emission properties in organic fluorophores: toward larger bathochromic shifts, enhanced molar extinction coefficients, and greater stokes shifts
